topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The waiting room smelled faintly of antiseptic and old paper, a scent that grated on Elara’s nerves like sandpaper. She tapped her foot against the linoleum floor, the rhythmic thudding a sharp counterpoint to the hushed, professional murmur of the other patients. *Hurry up,* she thought, staring at the clock on the wall, the second hand crawling with agonizing slowness. She’d been here for forty minutes. Forty minutes of this forced calm, this delicate unraveling of emotions, while she had actual things to do. When the door finally opened, she braced herself for some saccharine, overly gentle demeanor. Instead, a man with sharp, assessing eyes and a voice that held an unexpected, familiar edge greeted her. It was Marcus. The boy who used to dismantle her confidence with casual cruelty in the schoolyard. He sat down across from her, his posture relaxed, a practiced ease radiating from him. "Elara," he said, his tone level, devoid of the usual sneer. "Thanks for coming in." She managed a tight nod, her jaw feeling suddenly too tight. She just wanted to get to the point, to finish this excruciating ordeal so she could leave.

[story 2]

Liam chewed on the inside of his cheek, the dry taste of anxiety coating his tongue. Every minute stretched into an eternity. He shifted in the uncomfortable chair, his gaze darting around the sterile office, cataloging exits and potential escape routes. He had been dreading this appointment, a monumental waste of precious time. He really needed to just get this over with. When the door swung open, he instinctively tensed, ready to spring up. But the figure who entered was unmistakable. It was Finn. The one who used to mock his clumsy attempts at sports, the architect of his early insecurities. Finn looked different now—calm, composed, wearing a tailored blazer instead of a faded band t-shirt. "Liam," Finn acknowledged, his voice smooth as polished stone. Liam didn't offer a greeting. He just leaned forward, his hands gripping his knees so tightly his knuckles were white. "Look," Liam began, his voice clipped and low, "I don't have much time for small talk. What’s the agenda?" He watched Finn’s expression, searching for any sign of the old mockery, but found only a quiet, unnerving attentiveness.

[story 3]

Maya paced the perimeter of the waiting area, her restlessness manifesting in a constant, restless shifting of her weight. The silence was oppressive, amplifying the frantic drumming in her chest. She felt a distinct urge to bolt, to just run out of this suffocating space. She checked her watch again; the minutes ticked by with infuriating deliberation. She really needed this session to conclude. When the therapist finally called her name, Maya stopped mid-stride, her posture rigid. Standing there was Alex. The boy who had once made her feel utterly small, whose words had carved deep grooves into her self-perception. He offered a small, almost hesitant smile, a stark contrast to the memory of his sharp-toothed grin. "Ready when you are, Maya," he murmured. She didn't relax. Instead, she crossed her arms tightly over her chest, her gaze challenging, demanding that he move past the preamble and address the core issue immediately.
